skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Information and Ideas > Central Ideas and Details
skills.description: Identify the main idea of a passage or the key details that support it.
common_traps: Choosing a detail instead of the central idea
Picking an answer that is too broad or too narrow

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Information and Ideas > Inferences
skills.description: Draw logical conclusions or inferences based on textual evidence. Includes 'most logically completes the text' questions.
common_traps: Going one step beyond what the text supports
Contradicting a key detail in the passage
Partially capturing the logic but distorting one component

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Information and Ideas > Command of Evidence > Textual Evidence
skills.description: Select the quotation that best supports a stated claim. The correct quote must satisfy ALL parts of the claim simultaneously.
common_traps: Choosing a quote that only supports half the claim
Picking a thematically related quote that misses the specific point

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Information and Ideas > Command of Evidence > Quantitative Evidence
skills.description: Interpret data from graphs, tables, or charts and select evidence that supports or weakens a claim.
common_traps: Misreading axis labels or scale
Choosing data that supports a related but different claim

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Craft and Structure > Words in Context
skills.description: Choose the most logical and precise word or phrase to complete a sentence. Heavily dependent on identifying logical relationships (contrast, cause-effect, support) using signal words.
common_traps: Picking a word that sounds plausible in isolation rather than tracking the sentence logic
Missing contrast signals like 'but,' 'however,' 'in contrast to,' 'although'
Missing support signals like 'because,' 'similarly,' 'therefore'

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Craft and Structure > Text Structure and Purpose
skills.description: Identify why an author included a specific detail or describe the overall structure of a passage. Asks 'why did the author include this?' not 'what does this say?'
common_traps: Grabbing a true detail and misattributing its purpose
Assuming a hypothesis was confirmed when it was actually contradicted
Inventing a claim the text does not support

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Craft and Structure > Cross-Text Connections
skills.description: Compare two related passages and identify how one author would respond to the other, or how the texts relate.
common_traps: Confusing which author made which argument
Overstating agreement or disagreement between the texts

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Expression of Ideas > Rhetorical Synthesis
skills.description: Given bullet-point notes, select the sentence that best accomplishes a specific stated goal. Must anchor to the exact stated goal.
common_traps: Using irrelevant details from the notes that don't serve the stated goal
Getting the logic backwards (e.g. defining a term instead of classifying something)
Vague answers that gesture at the goal but never complete it

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Expression of Ideas > Transitions
skills.description: Choose the transition word or phrase that best connects two sentences or clauses. Identify the logical relationship first (contrast, cause-effect, example, sequence, restatement).
common_traps: Picking a transition that sounds smooth but misrepresents the logical relationship
Confusing 'for instance' (example) with 'in other words' (restatement)
Confusing 'subsequently' (sequence) with 'conversely' (contrast)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Standard English Conventions > Boundaries
skills.description: Use correct punctuation to join or separate clauses: periods, semicolons, colons, commas, and dashes. Core rule: two independent clauses need a semicolon, period, or comma + coordinating conjunction.
common_traps: Comma splice: using a comma alone to join two independent clauses
Run-on: no punctuation between two independent clauses
Using a comma instead of a colon before an explanation or list
Misusing 'its' vs 'it's' (possessive vs contraction)
Using semicolon + however + comma when what follows is not an independent clause
key_rules: Colon: introduces an explanation, list, or elaboration; must be preceded by a complete independent clause
Semicolon: joins two independent clauses without a conjunction
Comma + FANBOYS: joins two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction
its = possessive pronoun (no apostrophe); it's = it is (contraction)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Reading and Writing > Standard English Conventions > Form, Structure, and Sense
skills.description: Correct grammar errors including subject-verb agreement, pronoun agreement, verb tense, and modifier placement.
common_traps: Long interrupting phrases that hide the true subject
Plural subject mistaken for singular due to nearby singular nouns
Possessive apostrophes added to plain plural nouns
Wrong verb tense that doesn't match the sentence's time frame
key_rules: Strip interrupting phrases to find the true subject before checking verb agreement
Plural subjects take plural verbs regardless of what appears nearby
Only add possessive apostrophe if the noun actually owns something in the sentence

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Algebra > Linear Equations in One Variable
skills.description: Solve and interpret linear equations with one unknown.
common_traps: Arithmetic errors when isolating the variable
Misinterpreting what the question is asking for (e.g. solving for x when asked for 2x)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Algebra > Linear Equations in Two Variables
skills.description: Build, solve, and interpret linear equations with two variables. Includes finding intercepts, slope, and interpreting constants in context.
common_traps: Plugging in x and y coordinates backwards when finding intercepts
Sign errors when distributing slope through a point
Misinterpreting the constant (b) in y = mx + b: it's the value of y when x = 0
Confusing what the slope vs. the constant represents in a real-world context
key_rules: Always rewrite in y = mx + b form before interpreting
The constant b = value of y when x = 0 (the 'starting amount' or 'leftover')
To find y-intercept: set x = 0; to find x-intercept: set y = 0

skills.skill: Linear Inequalities in One or Two Variables
skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Algebra > Linear Inequalities in One or Two Variables
skills.description: Solve and interpret inequalities; identify solution regions on a number line or coordinate plane.
common_traps: Forgetting to flip the inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative
Misidentifying shaded regions on a graph

skills.skill: Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Algebra > Systems of Linear Equations in Two Variables
skills.description: Solve systems by substitution or elimination; determine when systems have one, zero, or infinite solutions.
common_traps: Forgetting to simplify equations before comparing coefficients
No solution condition: matching coefficients on variables but different constants (parallel lines)
Infinite solutions condition: equations are identical (same line)
key_rules: No solution = parallel lines = same variable coefficients, different constants
Infinite solutions = same line = equations are multiples of each other
Always put both equations in the same standard form before comparing

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Advanced Math > Equivalent Expressions
skills.description: Rewrite algebraic expressions in equivalent forms, including combining rational expressions and factoring.
common_traps: Forgetting to find a common denominator before adding fractions with variables
Treating a whole term as already having the needed denominator
Arithmetic errors when distributing after finding common denominator
key_rules: To add a/b + c, rewrite c as (c*b)/b first to get common denominator
Factor out common terms in numerator after combining

skills.skill: Nonlinear Equations in One Variable and Systems of Equations
skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Advanced Math > Nonlinear Equations in One Variable
skills.description: Solve quadratic and other nonlinear equations; interpret solutions in context.
common_traps: Forgetting to check for extraneous solutions
Using the quadratic formula incorrectly (sign errors under the radical)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Advanced Math > Nonlinear Functions
skills.description: Interpret and rewrite exponential, quadratic, and other nonlinear functions. Includes rewriting exponential forms using exponent rules.
common_traps: Confusing (a^m)^n = a^(mn) with a^m * a^n = a^(m+n)
Not recognizing (1 + p/100) as a growth rate multiplier
Forgetting that x/4 exponent means taking the 1/4 power of the base
key_rules: (a^m)^n = a^(mn): use this to match exponential forms
(1 + p/100)^x is the standard compound growth form
To match two exponential forms, equate the bases after making exponents identical

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Percentages
skills.description: Calculate percent change, percent of a total, and interpret percent language precisely.
common_traps: 'Increased by X%' vs 'increased to X%': 'by' means original + X%, 'to' means the result IS X% of original
Forgetting to add the original when calculating 'increased by'
Converting percentages to decimals incorrectly
key_rules: Increased BY X%: new = original × (1 + X/100)
Increased TO X%: new = original × (X/100)
Percent change = (new - old) / old × 100

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Ratios, Rates, and Proportional Relationships
skills.description: Set up and solve proportions; interpret unit rates in context.
common_traps: Confusing a ratio of sides with a ratio of areas (area scales as the square of side ratio)
Setting up the proportion with mismatched units
key_rules: If sides scale by factor k, areas scale by k²
Always label units when setting up a proportion to catch mismatches

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Two-Variable Data
skills.description: Interpret scatterplots, lines of best fit, and apply transformations to data sets.
common_traps: When multiplying all y-values by a constant, forgetting to scale both the slope AND the y-intercept
When adding a constant to all y-values, forgetting that only the intercept shifts (slope unchanged)
Misreading the y-intercept or slope from a graph
key_rules: Multiply all y by k: new slope = k × old slope; new intercept = k × old intercept
Add constant c to all y: new intercept = old intercept + c; slope unchanged

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > One-Variable Data
skills.description: Interpret mean, median, mode, range, and standard deviation; understand how changes to data affect these measures.
common_traps: Confusing mean with median, especially in skewed distributions
Misidentifying the effect of an outlier on mean vs. median

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Probability and Conditional Probability
skills.description: Calculate basic and conditional probabilities from tables or descriptions.
common_traps: Using the total population instead of the conditional subgroup as the denominator
Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A)

skills.skill: Inference from Sample Statistics and Margin of Error
skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Inference from Sample Statistics
skills.description: Evaluate whether sample statistics can be generalized to a population; interpret margin of error.
common_traps: Overgeneralizing from a non-random or non-representative sample
Confusing margin of error with the sample statistic itself

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Problem-Solving and Data Analysis > Evaluating Statistical Claims
skills.description: Assess whether a conclusion is supported by data; distinguish correlation from causation.
common_traps: Assuming causation from a correlation
Accepting a conclusion that goes beyond what the data supports

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Geometry and Trigonometry > Area and Volume
skills.description: Calculate area and surface area of 2D shapes and volume of 3D shapes.
common_traps: Forgetting to subtract missing faces (e.g. a lidless box has 5 faces, not 6)
Confusing area formula with volume formula
Using diameter instead of radius in circle formulas
key_rules: Surface area: identify all faces, check for missing ones, sum their areas
Circle area = πr²; circumference = 2πr (r = radius, not diameter)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Geometry and Trigonometry > Lines, Angles, and Triangles
skills.description: Apply properties of parallel lines, triangle similarity/congruence, Pythagorean theorem, and special triangles.
common_traps: Forgetting to use the Pythagorean theorem to find a missing side before setting up ratios
Setting up similarity ratios with non-corresponding sides
Forgetting that the hypotenuse is the side across from the right angle
In circles: forgetting that two radii to points on the circle form an isoceles triangle
key_rules: Similar triangles: identify shared angles first, then set up ratios of corresponding sides
When the right angle is at the center of a circle with both legs as radii: AB = r√2
Distance formula to find radius: r = √((x2-x1)² + (y2-y1)²)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Geometry and Trigonometry > Right Triangles and Trigonometry
skills.description: Apply sine, cosine, and tangent ratios; use special right triangles (30-60-90, 45-45-90).
common_traps: Mixing up which side is opposite vs. adjacent
Forgetting the complementary angle identity: sin(x) = cos(90-x)

skills.khan_academy_path: SAT Math > Geometry and Trigonometry > Circles
skills.description: Work with arc length, sector area, central angles, inscribed angles, and circle equations.
common_traps: Confusing central angle with inscribed angle (inscribed = half the central for same arc)
Using diameter instead of radius in the circle equation (x-h)² + (y-k)² = r²
